critique_points.point_001: The paper lacks direct comparison to recent prompt tuning or prefix tuning methods designed for frozen LLMs, weakening the argument for the novelty and advantage of P-Adapters.
critique_points.point_002: There is no justification or ablation study for placing P-Adapters solely between the embedding layer and the first self-attention layer, leaving the architectural choice unexplored.
critique_points.point_003: The method relies on predicting single object tokens using object labels as supervision, which may limit its applicability to tasks without such annotations.
critique_points.point_004: Experiments do not include fine-tuning baselines or comparisons to full model fine-tuning, making it unclear how much improvement is due to parameter efficiency versus factual knowledge enhancement.
critique_points.point_005: Evaluation metrics focus mainly on Precision@1 and consistency but omit recall, precision at k>1, or confidence calibration, which could provide a fuller picture of model robustness.
critique_points.point_006: The dataset and fact types tested are limited to LAMA with 41 Wikidata relations, raising questions about generalization to other knowledge bases, languages, or prompt styles.
critique_points.point_007: There is no comparison to simple but effective baselines such as majority voting or ensemble prompting over multiple paraphrases, which could challenge the reported gains.
critique_points.point_008: Important training hyperparameters for P-Adapters, such as learning rate, number of epochs, batch size, optimizer, and early stopping criteria, are missing, hindering reproducibility.
critique_points.point_009: The sources and handling of relation and subject annotations used in Mixture of Experts and oracle baselines are not described, limiting replication and fair assessment.
critique_points.point_010: The description of the Mixture of Experts model is ambiguous regarding expert routing training, number of experts, and mapping from natural language to continuous prompts, reducing clarity.
critique_points.point_011: The paper does not quantify the parameter count or computational overhead introduced by P-Adapters nor discuss how model size affects their efficiency or effectiveness.
critique_points.point_012: No evaluation is performed on different languages, prompt styles beyond templated prompts, or other LLM architectures such as generative models, limiting claims of generalizability.
critique_points.point_013: Handling of ambiguous or multi-answer facts is not addressed, which is important as many real-world facts have multiple valid answers or require context.
critique_points.point_014: The influence of the consistency loss term is only lightly explored and no alternative loss functions or training regimes are considered to potentially improve accuracy-consistency tradeoffs.
critique_points.point_015: The mapping from predicted masked tokens to object labels is insufficiently detailed, especially regarding multi-token objects or decoding strategies beyond argmax.

summary: This paper proposes P-Adapters, lightweight neural adapters inserted between the embedding layer and the first self-attention layer of frozen large language models, trained to transform variable natural language prompts into stable continuous prompts to improve the consistency and accuracy of factual extraction. Evaluated on the LAMA dataset across diverse out-of-domain settings and models like BERT and RoBERTa, P-Adapters significantly improve precision and consistency without requiring relation or subject annotations, unlike Mixture of Experts baselines. The work demonstrates that preserving the original subject entity embeddings is critical to performance and that P-Adapters provide a practical, annotation-light approach to robust knowledge extraction from frozen LLMs.
strengths: Introduces a novel, lightweight adapter architecture that enhances factual extraction robustness without additional annotations or fine-tuning of the full model.
Provides extensive empirical evaluation across multiple datasets, language models, and out-of-distribution settings, demonstrating consistent accuracy and consistency gains.
weaknesses: Lacks direct comparison to recent prompt tuning methods and fine-tuning baselines, limiting understanding of relative advantages.
Insufficient methodological justifications, incomplete hyperparameter details, and limited evaluation scope reduce reproducibility and generalizability.
questions: How would P-Adapters perform compared to recent prompt tuning or prefix tuning methods applied to frozen LLMs on the same tasks?
Can the authors clarify the decision to place adapters only at the embedding layer and whether multiple or deeper adapter placements were considered or tested?
title: P-Adapters: Robustly Extracting Factual Information from Language Models with Diverse Prompts
